message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Introduction</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>To support the government response to the coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) pandemic, accessible and sustainable testing approaches are needed. Private retail pharmacies are a key channel through which communities can access COVID-19 testing. We examined the level and determinants of the willingness to pay (WTP) for rapid COVID-19 testing delivered through private retail pharmacies in Kenya.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Methods</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Data was collected following a cross-sectional double-bounded dichotomous choice contingent valuation survey across 341 clients visiting five private retail pharmacies in Nairobi, Kisumu and Siaya counties.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Our findings indicate mean and median WTP levels of KES 611 (US$ 5.59) and KES 506 (US$ 4.63), respectively. Estimated WTP varied across counties and increased with household income and self-reported interest in pharmacy-based COVID-19 rapid testing.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusion</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>These findings can inform price setting, price differentiation, price subsidization and other program design features geared towards enhancing affordability, equity, and uptake.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Key Questions</jats:title>
                  <jats:sec>
                    <jats:title>What is already known?</jats:title>
                    <jats:list list-type="bullet">
                      <jats:list-item>
                        <jats:p>The Coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) global pandemic continues to cause great morbidity, mortality, social and economic burden.</jats:p>
                      </jats:list-item>
                      <jats:list-item>
                        <jats:p>Pharmacies in Kenya have been involved in the delivery of several health interventions, such as malaria rapid testing, HIV self-testing, and other disease screening services.</jats:p>
                      </jats:list-item>
                      <jats:list-item>
                        <jats:p>While COVID-19 testing remains an important response strategy to the ongoing COVID-19 pandemic, it is not clear how much pharmacy clients in Kenya and similar settings would be willing to pay (WTP) to obtain rapid COVID-19 testing at pharmacies</jats:p>
                      </jats:list-item>
                    </jats:list>
                  </jats:sec>
                  <jats:sec>
                    <jats:title>What are the new findings?</jats:title>
                    <jats:list list-type="bullet">
                      <jats:list-item>
                        <jats:p>The mean and median willingness to pay (WTP) for a rapid test delivered at a private retail pharmacy was KES 611 (US$ 5.59) and KES 506 (US$ 4.63), respectively.</jats:p>
                      </jats:list-item>
                      <jats:list-item>
                        <jats:p>WTP varied by county, hence, the need for county-specific price-setting for pharmacy-based COVID-19 testing.</jats:p>
                      </jats:list-item>
                      <jats:list-item>
                        <jats:p>WTP increased with household income and interest in getting the COVID-19 test at a private retail pharmacy.</jats:p>
                      </jats:list-item>
                    </jats:list>
                  </jats:sec>
                  <jats:sec>
                    <jats:title>What do the new findings imply?</jats:title>
                    <jats:list list-type="bullet">
                      <jats:list-item>
                        <jats:p>A better understanding of the user’s willingness to pay price that can guide price setting, price differentiation, price subsidization and other program design features geared towards enhancing affordability, equity, and uptake.</jats:p>
                      </jats:list-item>
                    </jats:list>
                  </jats:sec>
                </jats:sec>
title: Level and determinants of willingness to pay for rapid COVID-19 testing delivered through private retail pharmacies in Kenya
